content.text: Real-world data is almost never flat — API responses contain objects inside objects, arrays of objects, and objects holding arrays. Nested destructuring lets you reach into these multi-level structures and extract deeply buried values in a single expression, using the same syntax rules as basic destructuring but applied recursively at each level.

Nested destructuring is the practice of writing destructuring patterns inside other destructuring patterns. The outer pattern extracts a value from the top level, and instead of binding it to a plain variable, you immediately apply another pattern to it — pulling out properties or elements from within it. There is no new syntax to learn: it is just the same object
patterns composed together, as deeply as needed.

The most common case is an object whose property value is itself another object. To destructure a nested object, replace the variable name with another set of curly braces containing the properties you want from that inner object:
to whatever value you find there." The word
itself is never assigned to a variable.
When an object property holds an array, replace the variable name with square brackets to destructure the array's elements directly:
The same approach works for API responses where a payload wraps an array of results:
When an array contains objects, combine array and object patterns. Use square brackets to position into the array, then curly braces to extract properties from the object at that position:
Default values work at every level of a nested pattern. You can provide defaults for both the intermediate container and the inner properties:
after the nested pattern tells JavaScript: "if

from that." Without this guard, missing containers crash with a TypeError.
Default values can also guard nested arrays:
React components frequently receive complex prop objects. Nested destructuring in the parameter keeps the JSX clean without repeated dot-access:
When API data is stored in state, nested destructuring in a render or effect extracts exactly the fields you need without cluttering the component body with intermediate variables:

Nested destructuring is powerful but has a readability ceiling. More than two or three levels deep in a single expression becomes hard to parse at a glance. When a pattern grows unwieldy, break it into sequential steps — each destructuring step remains simple and the code stays readable:
Sequential destructuring also makes debugging easier because you can inspect each intermediate level with
Forgetting the container default causes a crash
Thinking the container key becomes a variable

Combining four or more levels of nesting in a single destructuring pattern makes the code difficult to read, review, and debug. The rule of thumb is: if you cannot read the pattern in one glance, split it into two steps.
Always guard missing nested containers with a default.
Any time the intermediate object or array might be absent — which is common with optional API fields — add
to prevent a TypeError at runtime.
Limit inline nesting to two levels.

One level of nesting (an object inside an object, or an array inside an object) is easy to follow. A second level is manageable. A third level inline is where readability drops sharply — prefer sequential steps.
Use renamed variables to document what the value represents.
When a property name is generic (like
appearing at multiple levels), rename it to clarify context:
Do not nest destructure an array you will also iterate.
If you need to map over all items in an array, extract it as a whole variable first (
), rather than destructuring individual elements and losing the rest.
patterns together — any pattern can appear wherever a variable name would go.
) to avoid collisions and improve clarity.
Deeper nesting should be broken into sequential destructuring statements for readability and debuggability.

Understanding nested destructuring is what allows React developers to write concise components that work with realistic, multi-level API data — expressing all required extractions in the parameter signature rather than scattering property access throughout the function body.
